text: The surgeon identifies the ureter as the medial border of the parorectal space, highlighting its importance as a landmark during the laparoscopic resection procedure.
The uterine vessels are visible crossing the medial aspect of the surgical field along their tract.
The surgeon carefully opens the parorectal space, staying lateral to the obliterated umbilical artery to avoid damaging the ureter.
The external iliac vessels are visible as the lateral border of the surgical field during the laparoscopic resection of the pelvic sidewall or obturator lymph nodes.
The levator ani muscle is visible forming the base of the parorectal space, with the parietal fascia covering the obturator muscle.
The surgeon identifies the internus muscle, which forms the lateral aspect of the parorectal space during the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon navigates the parorectal space, ensuring to stay lateral to the obliterated umbilical artery to prevent damage to the ureter, which forms the medial border of the space.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the pelvic sidewall, resecting the obturator lymph nodes while maintaining awareness of surrounding anatomical structures.
The rectum remains in situ as the surgeon performs dissection in the Total Mesorectal Excision (TME) plane, carefully separating the mesorectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon visualizes a large metastatic lymph node within the left pelvic sidewall, highlighting its distinct appearance against surrounding tissues.
The surgeon opens medially from the ureter to access the lymph node, aiming to avoid a wider dissection of the parorectal space and prevent damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the parorectal space due to the size and location of the lymph node, exposing surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon dissects lateral to the ureter to enhance visualization of the parorectal structures, carefully maneuvering around the anatomical landmarks.
The video provides an overview of the parorectal structures, highlighting their spatial arrangement and visibility within the surgical field.
The surgeon safely dissects parallel to the external iliac artery, further opening the parorectal space and revealing surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection distally, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels as they emerge in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a branch from the external iliac vein, highlighting anatomical variance in the parorectal region.

The surgeon identifies a vein branching from the external iliac vein, which appears to be an aberrant or accessory vein. This vein is the confluence of the superior physical vein and the obturator vein, typically ending in the internal iliac vein.
The obturator internus fascia shows a fascial thickening where the levator ani muscle attaches, forming the base of the parorectal space.
The surgeon carefully dissects the pelvic sidewall to mobilize an enlarged lymph node, revealing anatomical structures and variations in the area.
The surgeon carefully separates the vein branching, which is closely related to the obturator nerve, ensuring precise dissection to avoid nerve damage.

The surgeon achieves increased mobility of the malignant lymph node, providing a clear view of the parorectal space and its anatomical structures, including the levator ani muscle and pelvic parietal fascia.
The surgeon places the completely mobilized lymph node into an extraction bag, preparing it for removal alongside the rectal specimen.
Following the lymph node dissection, the surgeon completes the rectal resection laparoscopically, utilizing minimally invasive techniques to remove the rectal specimen.
